body:
All this logic was merged into `setProxy`. This is the key to re-evaluating the environment variables on redirects.

Oh, ... and it was replaced with a single call to the [`proxy-from-env`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/proxy-from-env) library (a [single line](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/4436/files?w=1#diff-586c04c24608b86a074073c5514cc195be5c4c87ddb56da233f6d998ada4159fR31)).
author: mbargiel

body:
To use the right protocol defined by environment variable-set proxies on redirect, `setProxy` now overrides the `options.protocol` when needed. That allows us to simplify this logic a bit, but requires moving the `options.agent` assignment to after we set the proxy.

By the way, is it me or is the `agent` only necessary when `maxRedirects` is set to 0? Unless I'm mistaken, `httpsFollow/httpFollow` use `agents.https`/`agents.http`.
author: mbargiel

body: I changed this because this was failing with an error in `follow-redirects`. This can be considered a breaking change if, previously, users could provide either `http` or `https` without a trailing colon. If you want to preserve the ability to not specify the `:`, let me know and I'll adjust `setProxy` to normalize it as needed, when `options.protocol` is overridden.
author: mbargiel

body: I (somewhat hackily) re-used the proxy as both a proxy and the redirected endpoint. That saved me from starting another server to handle the redirection and having to deal with that extra server in `afterEach`. It would be cleaner, but that's for a single test. Let me know what you prefer.
author: mbargiel

body:
This `headers.host` assignment was done two different ways:
- When initializing the request: https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/4436/files?w=1#diff-586c04c24608b86a074073c5514cc195be5c4c87ddb56da233f6d998ada4159fL216
- When following redirects: https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/4436/files?w=1#diff-586c04c24608b86a074073c5514cc195be5c4c87ddb56da233f6d998ada4159fL41

I moved this here because it's now possible to have different effective proxying on redirects when re-resolving the proxy from environment variables - ie set for `http_proxy` but not set for `https_proxy`, or skipping due to `no_proxy` for one call but not a redirect, etc.

body:
Added tests are excellent and valuable 🙌. 

At present I suggest we don't recommend auto-serialization of form-data as the default method of choice. Instead, let's move it to be after the manual section and say that we support it now. After we have more tests, and after it is well accepted by the community, let's adapt and recommend it by default. 
Currently, conversion of files in node will not work. This is solved by https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/4469 but tests and feedback are needed in this area. 

This Pr goes way beyond adding tests to FormData payload as the title suggest. It includes FormData documentation, a whole section of documentation about AbortController, adding import from "form-data" to "Node" only, and what seems like unrelated changes to scripts (`cross-env`). I guess it is obvious why this is less than ideal. 
While the contribution is great it would be even better if it will be separated to PRs that are scoped to a certain topic/domain.

body:
What about https over https? You still need a tunnel in that case because you need end-to-end encryption with the intended recipient.

Just for reference, here's how I fixed HTTP CONNECT support for secure HTTPS proxying. My solution   relies on the underlying agents (I mentioned it [here](https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/4318#issuecomment-1176692529) but I found it a bit too hacky and it required introducing an unmaintained dependency, which I wasn't comfortable with). 

You could also check out my fork directly for ideas : https://github.com/mbargiel/axios/compare/e6f9026d51d5e1386bc6935e563d95c375c45d02...feature/fix-proxy/http-connect
author: mbargiel

body: The nice thing with agents is that you don't have to worry about problems with follow-redirects: the tunnel is established in a lower abstraction layer, and once Axios has the socket, it's established properly to the end server. Redirects work over the tunnel.

body:
I assume that `data` is `undefined` in the scenario? Would it not be better to just check that then?

```
(typeof data === 'undefined' || data && data.length === 0)
```

Feels safer than making specific checks against method? I mean, that is the actual issue, right? If no content; Do not decode.
author: lejahmie

body:
Thanks for your reply, yes indeed this is the case.

However the problem is that `data` is always `undefined` for requests without a body and would also skip the all decompression on those. (e.g `GET` request without a body)
As far as I could comprehend, `data` is only set before hand if it is a `POST` request. (EDIT: or any request with a body)

The response stream is handled after this `data` variable is checked. If you would include this `undefined` check the unit tests 
 - should support gunzip error handling
 - should support transparent gunzip

will fail as they would not decompress the response anymore.

## Pull Request Overview

This pull request adds support for React Native blob objects to enable proper handling of file uploads in React Native environments. React Native uses a special "blob" object structure with a `uri` field that differs from standard web Blob objects.

Key changes:
- Added two new utility functions `isReactNativeBlob` and `isReactNative` for detecting React Native blob objects and environment
- Modified `toFormData` helper to handle React Native blobs by checking for React Native FormData and blob objects before processing

| lib/helpers/toFormData.js | Added conditional logic to append React Native blob objects directly when in a React Native environment |

<!-- metadata:{"confidence":8,"steps":[{"text":"Looking at the code change, I need to analyze the condition placement and logic for handling React Native blobs. Let me examine the utility functions being used.","toolCalls":[{"toolName":"bash","input":{"command":"rg -n isReactNative lib/utils.js -A 5"}},{"toolName":"bash","input":{"command":"rg -n isReactNativeBlob lib/utils.js -A 5"}}]},{"text":"","toolCalls":[{"toolName":"think","input":{"thought":"Now I understand the utility functions:

1. `isReactNativeBlob(value)` - returns true if value has a `uri` property
2. `isReactNative(formData)` - returns true if formData has `getParts` method (indicating React Native's FormData)

Looking at the diff again:
```javascript
} else if (utils.isReactNative(formData) && utils.isReactNativeBlob(value)) {
  formData.append(renderKey(path, key, dots), convertValue(value));
  return false;
}
```

This is placed inside the block:
```javascript
if (value && !path && typeof value === 'object') {
```

The issue is the `!path` condition. When `path` is truthy (an array for nested properties), this entire block is skipped. So nested React Native blobs won't be caught by this new condition.

For nested blobs, the code would fall through to:
```javascript
if (isVisitable(value)) {
  return true;
}
```

Since `isVisitable` checks for plain objects (`utils.isPlainObject(thing) || utils.isArray(thing)`), and a React Native blob like `{ uri: 'file://...' }` is a plain object, it would return `true`, causing unwanted recursion into the blob's properties.

This is a legitimate bug in the implementation - nested React Native blobs won't work correctly.

However, looking at existing patterns in the codebase - other special handling like arrays and file lists are also only at the root level. But those cases make sense because arrays get flattened differently at root.

For React Native blobs though, they should be handled at any level. The blob should be appended directly regardless of nesting depth.

Actually, wait - let me look at the condition more carefully. The check `!path` means this only runs when `path` is falsy. Looking at how the code works:

1. First call: `build(obj)` - path is undefined
2. In forEach, visitor is called with path (undefined at first level)
3. For nested: `build(el, path ? path.concat(key) : [key])` - path becomes an array

So at the first level of properties, `path` is `undefined`, making `!path` true. For nested properties, `path` is an array like `['nestedKey']`, making `!path` false.

This means the React Native blob handling only works at the first nesting level, not for deeply nested blobs.

This is a real bug, but let me consider severity:
- It's a new feature, so the bug is in new functionality
- Nested blobs won't be handled - they'll be recursed into incorrectly
- This could cause data loss or malformed form data

However, I should also check if this is really a bug or intentional. Looking at the PR description, it says it's adding support for React Native blobs that have a `uri` field. The React Native FormData can detect and upload them directly from filesystem.

The bug is real: the condition placement inside `!path` block limits the functionality to only root-level blobs, not nested ones. The fix would be to either:
1. Move the condition outside the `if (value && !path && typeof value === 'object')` block, or
2. Add a similar check after the block

Actually, looking more carefully at the logic flow - after the big if block, there's:
```javascript
if (isVisitable(value)) {

formData.append(renderKey(path, key, dots), convertValue(value));
```

1. The `if (value && !path && typeof value === 'object')` block is skipped
2. `isVisitable({ uri: '...' })` returns true (it's a plain object)
3. So it returns true, causing recursion into the blob object
4. This would try to append the `uri` string property instead of the blob object

This is definitely a bug. The React Native blob check should happen before the `isVisitable` check, regardless of the path level.

## Pull Request Overview

This PR attempts to address issue #5888 by adding fallback values for potentially undefined zlib constants. However, the implementation has several significant issues.

**Key Changes:**
- Adds fallback value `|| 2` to `BROTLI_OPERATION_FLUSH` constant references in `brotliOptions` object

**Issues Identified:**
1. **Mismatch between description and implementation**: The PR description describes fixing `Z_SYNC_FLUSH` in `zlibOptions`, but the code only modifies `BROTLI_OPERATION_FLUSH` in `brotliOptions`
2. **Questionable approach**: Using hardcoded fallback values masks underlying environmental issues rather than addressing root causes
3. **Redundant with existing checks**: The code already has `isBrotliSupported` checks that guard Brotli usage
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The hardcoded fallback value of `2` may not be the correct value for `BROTLI_OPERATION_FLUSH`. In Node.js's zlib module, `BROTLI_OPERATION_FLUSH` has a value of `2`, but relying on a hardcoded value is fragile and could break if the constant's value changes or differs across Node.js versions.

More importantly, if `zlib.constants.BROTLI_OPERATION_FLUSH` is undefined, it indicates that either:
1. The Node.js version doesn't support Brotli (added in Node.js 10.16.0)
2. There's a deeper environmental issue with the zlib module

The code already has a check for Brotli support on line 38 (`isBrotliSupported`). A better approach would be to either:
- Rely on that existing check to prevent using brotli when it's not supported
- Guard the constant access with a proper check rather than using a fallback

Consider removing the `|| 2` fallback or implementing a more robust check for Brotli support.

## Pull Request Overview

This PR addresses compatibility with frozen prototypes (Hardened JS/SES environments) by replacing direct property assignments with `Object.defineProperty()` calls to avoid the JavaScript "override mistake" that causes failures when prototypes are frozen.

**Key Changes:**
- Modified `extend()` to use `Object.defineProperty()` instead of direct assignment
- Updated `inherits()` to define the constructor property using `Object.defineProperty()`
- Refactored `reduceDescriptors()` to use spread operator instead of direct assignment
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The property descriptors are missing critical attributes. When using `Object.defineProperty()`, unspecified attributes default to `false`, which differs from direct assignment behavior where they default to `true`. This means properties will not be writable, enumerable, or configurable.

The descriptor should be:
```javascript
Object.defineProperty(a, key, {
  value: bind(val, thisArg),
  writable: true,
  enumerable: true,
  configurable: true
});
```

This applies to both branches of the conditional to maintain compatibility with the original behavior of direct property assignment.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The `constructor` property descriptor is missing critical attributes. When using `Object.defineProperty()`, unspecified attributes default to `false`, which differs from direct assignment behavior. The `constructor` property should be writable and configurable to match standard JavaScript prototype behavior.

The descriptor should be:
```javascript
Object.defineProperty(constructor.prototype, 'constructor', {
  value: constructor,
  writable: true,
  enumerable: false,
  configurable: true
});
```

Note: `enumerable: false` is correct for constructor properties (this matches the standard behavior), but `writable` and `configurable` should be `true`.
```suggestion
    value: constructor,
    writable: true,
    enumerable: false,
    configurable: true
```

The `reducedDescriptors` object is used as the second argument to `Object.defineProperties(obj, reducedDescriptors)` on line 604, which expects an object where each property value is a property descriptor object (with keys like `value`, `writable`, `get`, `set`, etc.).

By wrapping the descriptor in `Object.defineProperty`, you're creating a data property where `reducedDescriptors[name].value` equals the descriptor, instead of `reducedDescriptors[name]` being the descriptor itself.

The original assignment `reducedDescriptors[name] = ret || descriptor;` should be kept as-is. The `reducedDescriptors` object is a plain object being built up, not a frozen/sealed prototype, so the original assignment will work correctly.
```suggestion
      reducedDescriptors[name] = ret || descriptor;
```

## Pull Request Overview

This PR attempts to fix issue #4537 by changing how errors are handled in interceptors - making the `onRejected` handler execute in the same interceptor where the error occurs, rather than in the next interceptor. The PR also addresses a TypeError that occurred when config was lost during the promise chain.

Key changes:
- Modified asynchronous request interceptor chain execution to use `.then().catch()` pattern with config preservation logic
- Modified response interceptor chain to use `.then().catch()` pattern instead of `.then(fulfilled, rejected)`
- Added three test cases to verify error handling behavior in interceptors

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 7 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| lib/core/Axios.js | Modified promise chain logic for both request and response interceptors to handle errors in the same interceptor, including addition of config preservation mechanism |
| test/specs/interceptors.spec.js | Added three new test cases covering error handling in request/response interceptors and config modification behavior when errors occur |
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The test logic is flawed. The test expects both `rejectedSpy1` and `rejectedSpy2` to be called, but based on the interceptor execution order (reversed for request interceptors), the second interceptor (with `rejectedSpy2`) will execute first and throw `error2`. If the error is handled in the same interceptor as intended by this PR, `rejectedSpy2` should be called with `error2`, but the first interceptor (with `rejectedSpy1`) should never execute because the promise chain would be rejected.

The test needs to clarify the expected behavior: should the request continue after an error is handled, or should it stop? The current assertions don't match either scenario correctly.
```suggestion
      expect(rejectedSpy1).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Changing from `promise.then(fulfilled, rejected)` to `promise.then(fulfilled).catch(rejected)` changes the error handling semantics. In the original pattern, if `rejected` handler throws an error, it propagates immediately. In the new pattern, the error from `rejected` is caught by a subsequent `.catch()` in the next iteration.

However, unlike the request interceptor change, this change doesn't have the problematic `prevResult` logic, so it might actually work as intended for making the error handler part of the same interceptor. But this needs to be consistent with the request interceptor implementation, and both should be carefully reviewed for correctness.
```suggestion
      promise = promise.then(responseInterceptorChain[i++], responseInterceptorChain[i++]);
```

body:
The promise chain logic has several critical issues:

1. **Falsy value handling**: Line 166 uses `result ? result : prevResult`, which will incorrectly fall back to `prevResult` for valid falsy values like `0`, `false`, `""`, or `null`. This should use `result !== undefined ? result : prevResult`.

2. **Lost error context**: The `.catch()` on line 167 consumes the error but then line 168 returns `prevResult`, which loses the error entirely. If an interceptor's `onRejected` handler throws or doesn't handle the error properly, the chain should propagate the error, not silently continue with the previous config.

3. **Incorrect chain behavior**: This implementation breaks the standard promise chain flow. After a `.catch()` returns successfully, the next `.then()` should receive that return value, not the `prevResult`.

The original implementation `promise.then(chain[i++], chain[i++])` properly passes both fulfilled and rejected values through the chain. The new implementation fundamentally changes this behavior in a way that breaks error propagation.

body:
After consideration,  I think we should just filter out some empty return values here.

According to the [docs](https://axios-http.com/docs/interceptors), every interceptor has a return value. For request interceptor, the return value should be a `config`. It can be identified by some specific properties. However, for response interceptors, the return value is user-defined. We cannot verify the result.

Therefore, I propose to choose a simple approach: filter out any return values that are `undefined`.
